subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Substitution (u-sub) works best when the integrand has a composite function and (all or part of) the derivative of the inner function is present.
Steps: (1) Pick u as the inner expression. (2) Compute du. (3) Rewrite the whole integral in u and du. (4) Integrate in u. (5) Substitute back to x.
Quick clue: something like f(g(x))¬∑g'(x).

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Integration by parts is used for products of functions.
Formula: ‚ąę u dv = u¬∑v ‚ąí ‚ąę v du.
Pick u using ILATE (Inverse, Log, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential). Then dv is the remaining factor.
Tip: choose u so that du becomes simpler.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Use partial fractions when integrating a rational function P(x)/Q(x) where deg(P) < deg(Q).
Steps: (1) Factor Q(x) into linear and/or irreducible quadratic factors. (2) Write the decomposition form. (3) Solve constants. (4) Integrate termwise using ln and arctan patterns.
Common mistake: forgetting repeated factors need higher powers in the decomposition.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
For integrals involving powers of sin and cos:
- If one power is odd, split one factor and convert the rest using sin¬≤+cos¬≤=1.
- If both are even, use half-angle identities: sin¬≤x=(1‚ąícos2x)/2, cos¬≤x=(1+cos2x)/2.
For tan/sec, use 1+tan¬≤x=sec¬≤x and sec¬≤x‚ąí1=tan¬≤x depending on what helps.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Trig substitution helps when you see square roots like ‚ąö(a¬≤‚ąíx¬≤), ‚ąö(a¬≤+x¬≤), ‚ąö(x¬≤‚ąía¬≤).
Typical choices:
- ‚ąö(a¬≤‚ąíx¬≤): x=a sinőł
- ‚ąö(a¬≤+x¬≤): x=a tanőł
- ‚ąö(x¬≤‚ąía¬≤): x=a secőł
Then simplify using trig identities and convert back at the end.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Useful properties:
1) ‚ąę_a^a f(x) dx = 0
2) ‚ąę_a^b f(x) dx = ‚ąí‚ąę_b^a f(x) dx
3) If f is even: ‚ąę_-a^a f(x) dx = 2‚ąę_0^a f(x) dx
4) If f is odd: ‚ąę_-a^a f(x) dx = 0
Also, ‚ąę_a^b f(x) dx + ‚ąę_b^c f(x) dx = ‚ąę_a^c f(x) dx.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
‚ąę x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) + C (n‚Č†‚ąí1)
‚ąę 1/x dx = ln|x| + C
‚ąę k dx = kx + C (k constant)
‚ąę (ax+b)^n dx = (ax+b)^(n+1)/(a(n+1)) + C (n‚Č†‚ąí1)

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
‚ąę e^x dx = e^x + C
‚ąę e^(ax) dx = (1/a)e^(ax) + C
‚ąę a^x dx = a^x/ln(a) + C (a>0, a‚Č†1)
‚ąę a^(kx) dx = a^(kx)/(k ln(a)) + C

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
‚ąę sin x dx = ‚ąícos x + C
‚ąę cos x dx = sin x + C
‚ąę sec^2 x dx = tan x + C
‚ąę csc^2 x dx = ‚ąícot x + C
‚ąę sec x tan x dx = sec x + C
‚ąę csc x cot x dx = ‚ąícsc x + C

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
‚ąę 1/(1+x^2) dx = tan^‚ąí1(x) + C
‚ąę 1/‚ąö(1‚ąíx^2) dx = sin^‚ąí1(x) + C
General forms:
‚ąę 1/(a^2+x^2) dx = (1/a) tan^‚ąí1(x/a) + C
‚ąę 1/‚ąö(a^2‚ąíx^2) dx = sin^‚ąí1(x/a) + C (a>0)

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: When you see (ax+b)^n, set u=ax+b so du=a dx. Convert to u^n and integrate using power rule. Remember to divide by a.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If the integrand contains g'(x) as a multiplier, try u=g(x). Example shape: g'(x)¬∑F(g(x)). Then ‚ąę g'(x)¬∑F(g(x)) dx becomes ‚ąę F(u) du.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: For x¬∑e^x (or x¬∑sinx, x¬∑cosx), choose u=x and dv=e^x dx. Then du=dx and v=e^x. Apply ‚ąęu dv=uv‚ąí‚ąęv du to reduce the product.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: For ‚ąę ln x dx, take u=ln x and dv=dx. Then du=1/x dx and v=x. Result: x ln x ‚ąí x + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
If Q(x) factors into distinct linear terms, decompose as A/(x‚ąía)+B/(x‚ąíb)+...
Then integrate each term: ‚ąę 1/(x‚ąía) dx = ln|x‚ąía| + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
For repeated linear factors like (x‚ąía)^2, use A/(x‚ąía) + B/(x‚ąía)^2 (and higher powers if needed).
Integrals: ‚ąę 1/(x‚ąía)^2 dx = ‚ąí1/(x‚ąía) + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
For an irreducible quadratic (ax^2+bx+c), use (Ax+B)/(ax^2+bx+c).
Often split into derivative part + remainder to get ln(...) and arctan(...) after completing the square.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If sin has an odd power: save one sin x, convert remaining sin¬≤ to 1‚ąícos¬≤, then let u=cos x (du=‚ąísin x dx).

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If cos has an odd power: save one cos x, convert remaining cos¬≤ to 1‚ąísin¬≤, then let u=sin x (du=cos x dx).

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
If both sin and cos powers are even, use half-angle:
sin¬≤x=(1‚ąícos2x)/2, cos¬≤x=(1+cos2x)/2 to reduce to integrals of cos(2x) and constants.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Sometimes use substitution x=a‚ąít in ‚ąę0^a f(x) dx:
Then ‚ąę0^a f(x) dx = ‚ąę0^a f(a‚ąít) dt. Adding both forms can simplify with symmetry.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę 2x¬∑cos(x^2) dx. Let u=x^2 ‚áí du=2x dx. Then integral = ‚ąę cos u du = sin u + C = sin(x^2) + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę (3x+1)^4 dx. Let u=3x+1 ‚áí du=3 dx ‚áí dx=du/3. Then integral = (1/3)‚ąęu^4 du = u^5/15 + C = (3x+1)^5/15 + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę x e^x dx. Take u=x, dv=e^x dx ‚áí du=dx, v=e^x. Then ‚ąę x e^x dx = x e^x ‚ąí ‚ąę e^x dx = x e^x ‚ąí e^x + C = e^x(x‚ąí1)+C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę x sin x dx. Take u=x, dv=sin x dx ‚áí du=dx, v=‚ąícos x. So integral = ‚ąíx cos x + ‚ąę cos x dx = ‚ąíx cos x + sin x + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę sin^3 x dx. Write sin^3 x = sin x(1‚ąícos^2 x). Let u=cos x ‚áí du=‚ąísin x dx. Then integral = ‚ąí‚ąę(1‚ąíu^2) du = ‚ąí(u ‚ąí u^3/3)+C = ‚ąícos x + (cos^3 x)/3 + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę 1/(x^2‚ąí1) dx. Factor: (x‚ąí1)(x+1). Decompose: 1/(x^2‚ąí1)=1/2¬∑[1/(x‚ąí1) ‚ąí 1/(x+1)]. Integrate: (1/2)(ln|x‚ąí1| ‚ąí ln|x+1|)+C = (1/2)ln|(x‚ąí1)/(x+1)| + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę_{-2}^{2} x^3 cos x dx. Here x^3 is odd and cos x is even, so product is odd. Integral over [-2,2] is 0.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Evaluate ‚ąę_{-ŌÄ}^{ŌÄ} cos^2 x dx. cos^2 x is even, so integral = 2‚ąę_{0}^{ŌÄ} cos^2 x dx = 2‚ąę_{0}^{ŌÄ} (1+cos2x)/2 dx = ‚ąę_0^ŌÄ (1+cos2x) dx = ŌÄ.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
ŗ¶łŗ¶ĺŗ¶¨ŗ¶łŗßćŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶Čŗ¶∂ŗ¶® ŗ¶™ŗ¶¶ŗßćŗ¶ßŗ¶§ŗ¶Ņ ŗ¶¨ŗßćŗ¶Įŗ¶¨ŗ¶Ļŗ¶ĺŗ¶į ŗ¶ēŗ¶įŗ¶ĺ ŗ¶Ļŗ¶Įŗ¶ľ ŗ¶Įŗ¶Ėŗ¶® ŗ¶áŗ¶®ŗßćŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶óŗßćŗ¶įŗßćŗ¶Įŗ¶ĺŗ¶®ŗßćŗ¶°ŗßá ŗ¶ĮŗßĆŗ¶óŗ¶Ņŗ¶ē ŗ¶ęŗ¶ĺŗ¶āŗ¶∂ŗ¶® ŗ¶•ŗ¶ĺŗ¶ēŗßá ŗ¶Źŗ¶¨ŗ¶ā ŗ¶≠ŗ¶Ņŗ¶§ŗ¶įŗßáŗ¶į ŗ¶Öŗ¶āŗ¶∂ŗßáŗ¶į ŗ¶°ŗßáŗ¶įŗ¶Ņŗ¶≠ŗßáŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶≠ (ŗ¶łŗ¶ģŗßćŗ¶™ŗßāŗ¶įŗßćŗ¶£ ŗ¶¨ŗ¶ĺ ŗ¶Üŗ¶āŗ¶∂ŗ¶Ņŗ¶ē) ŗ¶łŗ¶ĺŗ¶•ŗßá ŗ¶•ŗ¶ĺŗ¶ēŗßáŗ•§
ŗ¶ßŗ¶ĺŗ¶™: u = ŗ¶≠ŗ¶Ņŗ¶§ŗ¶įŗßáŗ¶į ŗ¶Öŗ¶āŗ¶∂, du ŗ¶¨ŗßáŗ¶į ŗ¶ēŗ¶įŗßč, ŗ¶łŗ¶ģŗßćŗ¶™ŗßāŗ¶įŗßćŗ¶£ ŗ¶áŗ¶®ŗßćŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶óŗßćŗ¶įŗ¶ĺŗ¶≤ŗ¶ēŗßá u-ŗ¶§ŗßá ŗ¶įŗßāŗ¶™ŗ¶ĺŗ¶®ŗßćŗ¶§ŗ¶į ŗ¶ēŗ¶įŗßč, ŗ¶áŗ¶®ŗßćŗ¶üŗ¶Ņŗ¶óŗßćŗ¶įŗßáŗ¶ü ŗ¶ēŗ¶įŗßč, ŗ¶∂ŗßáŗ¶∑ŗßá ŗ¶Üŗ¶¨ŗ¶ĺŗ¶į x-ŗ¶Ź ŗ¶ęŗ¶Ņŗ¶įŗßá ŗ¶Üŗ¶łŗßčŗ•§

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
By parts: ‚ąę u dv = uv ‚ąí ‚ąę v du.
u ŗ¶¨ŗ¶ĺŗ¶õŗ¶ĺŗ¶áŗ¶§ŗßá ILATE ŗ¶®ŗ¶Ņŗßüŗ¶ģ ŗ¶ēŗ¶ĺŗ¶úŗßá ŗ¶≤ŗ¶ĺŗ¶óŗßá: Inverse, Log, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
ŗ¶Įŗ¶¶ŗ¶Ņ f(x) even ŗ¶Ļŗ¶Įŗ¶ľ, ŗ¶§ŗ¶ĺŗ¶Ļŗ¶≤ŗßá ‚ąę_{-a}^{a} f(x) dx = 2‚ąę_{0}^{a} f(x) dx.
ŗ¶Įŗ¶¶ŗ¶Ņ f(x) odd ŗ¶Ļŗ¶Įŗ¶ľ, ŗ¶§ŗ¶ĺŗ¶Ļŗ¶≤ŗßá ‚ąę_{-a}^{a} f(x) dx = 0.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: For ‚ąę 1/(ax+b) dx, let u=ax+b. Result: (1/a) ln|ax+b| + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If you see ‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤), consider x=sinőł so ‚ąö(1‚ąíx¬≤)=cosőł. Then dx=cosőł dőł, which often simplifies.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If the integrand includes sec¬≤x, try substitution u=tan x because du=sec¬≤x dx.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If the integrand includes csc¬≤x, try u=cot x because du=‚ąícsc¬≤x dx.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: For integrals like ‚ąę e^x sin x dx or ‚ąę e^x cos x dx, apply by parts twice and solve for I (the same integral appears again).

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If deg(P) ‚Č• deg(Q), do polynomial long division first to convert to a polynomial + proper fraction, then decompose the proper part.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: When you see ‚ąö(tan x), first recognize it as a non-standard trig power. A safe first step is rewriting tan x = sin x / cos x so ‚ąö(tan x) = ‚ąösin x / ‚ąöcos x. This may help you express the integrand as powers of sin and cos. IMPORTANT: This rewriting does not guarantee an elementary solution. If no derivative-related factor like sec^2 x appears, the integral may be non-elementary/out-of-scope for standard HSC methods.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text:
Decision rule for integrals involving ‚ąö(tan x):
1) If the integrand contains sec^2 x (or can be rearranged to include sec^2 x), use u = tan x because du = sec^2 x dx. Example form: ‚ąę ‚ąö(tan x) sec^2 x dx.
2) If sec^2 x (or tan' x) is NOT present, do NOT force substitutions like u=‚ąö(tan x) as a guaranteed method. Instead, rewrite tan x = sin x/cos x and check if the expression becomes a standard sin-cos power integral.
3) If it still does not match standard HSC patterns, classify it as Non-elementary (advanced)/Out-of-scope and stop.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: If the integral is ‚ąę ‚ąö(tan x) sec¬≤x dx, use substitution u = tan x. Then du = sec¬≤x dx. The integral becomes ‚ąę ‚ąöu du = (2/3) u^(3/2) + C = (2/3)(tan x)^(3/2) + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Example: Evaluate ‚ąę ‚ąö(tan x) sec¬≤x dx. Let u = tan x ‚áí du = sec¬≤x dx. Then integral = ‚ąę ‚ąöu du = ‚ąę u^(1/2) du = (2/3)u^(3/2) + C = (2/3)(tan x)^(3/2) + C.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Integrals involving ‚ąö(tan x) are considered non-standard because they do not directly match basic trigonometric integrals. The key idea is to rewrite tan x into sine and cosine or to use substitution if sec¬≤x or derivative-related terms are present. Recognition and algebraic manipulation are crucial.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: The integral ‚ąę ‚ąö(tan x) dx does not have a simple elementary closed-form solution. It can be transformed using substitution, but it does not reduce to standard basic integrals typically taught in HSC level. In HSC context, such integrals usually appear with sec¬≤x or derivative-related factors.

subject: HSC Higher Math
text: Standard result: ‚ąę sec x dx = ln|sec x + tan x| + C. This formula is useful when the integrand can be rearranged into sec x (sec x + tan x)/(sec x + tan x) form.
